title: Groupoids and the Algebra of Rewriting in Group Presentations
overall_summary: The paper builds and studies a reduced Squier complex Sqρ(P) associated to a group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩: its vertices are elements of the free group F(X), its edges are applications of defining relations in arbitrary left and right contexts, and its 2-cells commute non-overlapping applications of relations. The main technical point is that the fundamental groupoid of this complex has natural left and right whiskering actions by F(X), making it a regular and in fact monoidal groupoid; consequently the star at the identity is a crossed module over F(X). The paper gives an explicit generating set and presentation for this star group and identifies it with Whitehead's free crossed module associated to the presentation. For the original matching/cancellation problem, the most relevant ingredients are the Squier-complex encoding of independent local reductions, the use of square 2-cells to prove commutation of independent moves, and the normalization of paths to products of elementary moves based at the identity. The introduction also points to Pride's augmented Squier complex for handling overlaps in free reductions, which is especially relevant because quasi-reduced words exclude the elementary three-letter overlap ℓℓ^{-1}ℓ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a group, let R be a set, and let ω : R → Γ be any function. Then there exists a free crossed Γ-module on ω, unique up to isomorphism. Explicitly, take the free group F on the set R × Γ, give it the right Γ-action determined on generators by (r,u)^v = (r,uv), and define δ : F → Γ on generators by δ(r,u) = u^{-1}ω(r)u. Let K be the normal Γ-invariant subgroup generated by all Peiffer elements (r,u)^{-1}(s,v)^{-1}(r,u)(s, v u^{-1}ω(r)u), where r,s ∈ R and u,v ∈ Γ. Then δ induces a crossed module ∂ : F/K → Γ, and the map r ↦ class of (r,1) is the universal basis over ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction starts with the free group on all formal Γ-translates of the proposed basis elements. The map δ is forced by equivariance of a crossed module boundary: the translate (r,u) must have boundary u^{-1}ω(r)u. The listed Peiffer elements are precisely the relations needed to impose the crossed-module Peiffer identity t^{u∂} = u^{-1}tu in the quotient. The subgroup K is normal, Γ-invariant, and lies in ker δ, so δ descends to F/K and the Γ-action descends as well. The crossed-module axioms then follow directly from the definitions of δ and the imposed Peiffer relations. For the universal property, given any crossed Γ-module T′ with a function σ : R → T′ satisfying ∂′σ(r)=ω(r), send each generator (r,u) to σ(r)^u; the crossed-module axioms in T′ kill all Peiffer elements, so this map factors uniquely through F/K. Uniqueness follows because the Γ-translates of the basis generate F/K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ∂ : C → Γ be the free crossed Γ-module with basis map ρ : R → C, and let Q = coker ∂. Then the abelianization C^ab is a free Q-module on the image of the composite R → C → C^ab.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Whitehead for this fact. In the explicit construction of the free crossed module, C is generated by formal translates of basis elements by Γ. After abelianization, the Peiffer relations identify the effect of translating by elements of im ∂ with the trivial action, so the Γ-action factors through Q = Γ/im ∂. No further linear relations among the images of the basis elements are introduced. Thus C^ab is the free module over the quotient group Q generated by the images of R.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = ⟨X : R⟩ be a group presentation. Put F = F(X), let bρ : R → F send a relation pair (l,r) to l^{-1}r in F, and let N be the normal closure in F of the image of bρ, so that G = F/N is the presented group. Let ∂ : C(P) → F be the free crossed F-module on bρ, and let π(P)=ker ∂. Then C(P) is isomorphic as a group to π(P) × N. Its abelianization C(P)^ab is a free G-module, and the natural map π(P) → C(P)^ab is injective. Consequently there is a short exact sequence of G-modules 0 → π(P) → C(P)^ab → N^ab → 0, where N^ab is the relation module of the presentation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The image of ∂ is N, giving an exact sequence 1 → π(P) → C(P) → N → 1. Since N is a subgroup of the free group F, it is itself free, so this extension splits. The kernel π(P) of a crossed module is central in C(P), hence the split extension is a direct product π(P) × N. Abelianizing then keeps π(P) injectively, because centrality gives C(P)^ab ≅ π(P) × N^ab at the level relevant to the kernel inclusion. Proposition 1.3 identifies C(P)^ab as a free module over F/N = G. The induced boundary map on abelianizations has image N^ab and kernel π(P), yielding the displayed exact sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a semiregular groupoid: its vertex set V(G) is a monoid with identity e, and V(G) acts on morphisms from the left and right, written x⊲α and α⊳x, compatibly with domains, ranges, identities, and groupoid composition. For morphisms α,β ∈ G define two everywhere-defined products α∗β = (α⊳βd) ∘ (αr⊲β) and α⊛β = (αd⊲β) ∘ (α⊳βr), where d and r denote domain and range. Then each of ∗ and ⊛ makes G into a monoid with identity 1_e. The operation ∗ makes G into a strict monoidal groupoid with object monoid V(G) if and only if ∗ and ⊛ coincide. If V(G) is a group, so G is regular, then ∗ and ⊛ are group operations on G. In the regular case, the inverse of α with respect to ∗ is αr^{-1}⊲α°⊳αd^{-1}, where α° is the groupoid inverse, and the range map r : (G,∗) → V(G) is a group homomorphism; also star_e(G), the set of morphisms with domain e, is a subgroup of (G,∗).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For α∗β, the morphism α⊳βd has range (αr)(βd), while αr⊲β has domain (αr)(βd), so the groupoid composite is defined; similarly for α⊛β. Associativity is checked by expanding both (α∗β)∗γ and α∗(β∗γ) and repeatedly using the axioms that left and right actions distribute over groupoid composition and associate with multiplication in V(G). The identity calculation is direct: 1_e∗α = 1_{αd}∘α and α∗1_e = α∘1_{αr}; the same argument works for ⊛. The equality ∗=⊛ is exactly the interchange condition needed for the product on morphisms and the monoid product on vertices to define a strict monoidal groupoid. When V(G) is a group, the displayed formula for the ∗-inverse is verified by composing α with the whiskered groupoid inverse and using inverse elements in V(G); the formula for ⊛ is analogous. Finally, r(α∗β)=r(α)r(β), so r is a homomorphism, and if αd=βd=e then (α∗β)d=e, so star_e(G) is closed under ∗ and inverses.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a regular groupoid. The group V(G) acts on the group (G,∗) by automorphisms via α^q = q^{-1}⊲α⊳q for q ∈ V(G). With this action, the restricted range map r : star_e(G) → V(G) is a crossed module if and only if G is monoidal, meaning that the two products ∗ and ⊛ on G coincide.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as the standard connection between regular groupoids and crossed modules. The action formula preserves domains equal to e, because q^{-1}e q=e, and sends ranges by conjugation: r(α^q)=q^{-1}r(α)q, giving the equivariance crossed-module axiom. The remaining crossed-module Peiffer identity compares conjugation inside the ∗-group star_e(G) with the action by the range r(β). Expanding β^{-1}∗α∗β using the inverse formula from Proposition 2.1 and simplifying via the whiskering axioms shows that this identity is equivalent to the ability to interchange the two possible products α∗β and α⊛β. Conversely, if the Peiffer identity holds on the star, arbitrary morphisms can be whiskered into the star and the same calculation forces ∗=⊛ throughout G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.1 / Reduced Squier complex construction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩, where relation pairs (l,r) ∈ R are words over X∪X^{-1} and products are interpreted in the free group F(X), the reduced Squier complex Sqρ(P) is the following 2-complex. Its vertices are the elements of F(X). Its directed edges are tuples (p,l,r,q), with p,q ∈ F(X) and (l,r) ∈ R, starting at plq and ending at prq. Its 2-cells are attached for every pair of non-overlapping relation applications: for p,q,p′,q′ ∈ F(X) and (l,r),(l′,r′) ∈ R, the two paths (p,l,r, q p′ l′ q′) followed by (p r q p′, l′, r′, q′), and (p l q p′, l′, r′, q′) followed by (p,l,r, q p′ r′ q′), have the same endpoints and bound a 2-cell.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition rather than a theorem. The point of the construction is that an edge records applying one defining relation in a specified left and right context, while a 2-cell records the fact that two relation applications in disjoint subwords can be performed in either order. Because vertices are already elements of the free group F(X), the construction suppresses explicit free-reduction bookkeeping.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩, the fundamental groupoid π(Sqρ(P), F(X)) of the reduced Squier complex, with object set F(X), is a regular groupoid. The vertex group is F(X), and the left and right actions of F(X) on single edges are u⊲(p,l,r,q) = (up,l,r,q) and (p,l,r,q)⊳v = (p,l,r,qv), extended to edge paths and then to homotopy classes.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Multiplying the left context of an edge by u changes its initial and terminal vertices from plq and prq to uplq and uprq, so it defines a valid left action. Multiplying the right context by v similarly changes the initial and terminal vertices to plqv and prqv, defining a right action. These actions extend edgewise to paths because adjacent endpoints remain adjacent after the same context multiplication. They preserve the defining 2-cells of Sqρ(P), since whiskering a square for two non-overlapping relation applications by an element of F(X) gives another square of the same form. The compatibility axioms in the definition of a regular groupoid are then immediate from associativity and identity in F(X).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩, the regular groupoid π(Sqρ(P), F(X)) is monoidal: for all homotopy classes of paths α and β, the two products α∗β = (α⊳βd) ∘ (αr⊲β) and α⊛β = (αd⊲β) ∘ (α⊳βr) are equal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First consider single-edge paths α=(p,l,r,q) and β=(p′,l′,r′,q′). Then α∗β is the path (p,l,r, q p′ l′ q′) followed by (p r q p′, l′, r′, q′), while α⊛β is the path (p l q p′, l′, r′, q′) followed by (p,l,r, q p′ r′ q′). These are precisely the two sides of one defining 2-cell of Sqρ(P), so they are homotopic. For longer paths, the proof uses induction. Fix β to be one edge and induct on the length of α: split α into a prefix and its final edge, commute the final edge past β using the single-edge case, and then use the induction hypothesis for the prefix. Then induct on the length of β: split β into a prefix and its final edge, apply the induction hypothesis to commute α past the prefix, and use the already-proved single-edge-in-β case for the last edge. The whiskering actions distribute over path concatenation, so the algebraic expansions match the definitions of ∗ and ⊛ at each induction step.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = ⟨X : R⟩ be a group presentation. The set star_1(π(Sqρ(P),F(X))) of homotopy classes of paths in Sqρ(P) whose domain is the identity element 1 ∈ F(X) is a group under the product ∗. The range map r : star_1(π(Sqρ(P),F(X))) → F(X) is a crossed module, using the action α^q = q^{-1}⊲α⊳q of F(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 3.1 the fundamental groupoid is regular. Proposition 2.1 says that in a regular groupoid, the star at the vertex identity is a subgroup under ∗ and the range map is a group homomorphism. Theorem 3.2 says the groupoid is monoidal, and Proposition 2.2 then gives precisely the crossed-module axioms for the range map on the star.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S1 be the set of edges of Sqρ(P) whose domain is 1 ∈ F(X). Explicitly, S1 = {λ_{l,r,q} : q ∈ F(X), (l,r) ∈ R}, where λ_{l,r,q} = (q^{-1}l^{-1}, l, r, q). If e is any edge in the connected component of 1, define eλ = (ed)^{-1}⊲e ∈ S1. Then every edge path α in star_1(Sqρ(P)) is equal, under the ∗ product, to a product of elements of S1. Hence star_1(Sqρ(P)) is generated by S1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write α as a composable edge path α1∘α2∘...∘αn. For each edge set λ_i=(α_i d)^{-1}⊲α_i, which is based at 1 by construction. Since α starts at 1, λ_1=α_1. Assume by induction that the prefix α1∘...∘α_{n-1} equals λ_1∗...∗λ_{n-1}. The range of this prefix is α_n d, so multiplying by λ_n using ∗ gives prefix ∘ (α_n d⊲λ_n). But α_n d⊲λ_n = α_n by definition of λ_n. Thus the full path equals λ_1∗...∗λ_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If α∘β is an edge path in star_1(Sqρ(P)), and if γλ denotes the ∗-product of based edges associated to a path γ as in Proposition 3.4, then (α∘β)λ = αλ ∗ βλ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The list of single edges in the composite α∘β is the concatenation of the list of single edges in α and the list of single edges in β. Proposition 3.4 constructs γλ by translating each edge of γ individually to an edge based at 1 and then multiplying these translated edges in the original order using ∗. Applying this construction to the concatenated list gives exactly the ∗-product of the construction for α followed by the construction for β.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩, the group star_1(Sqρ(P)) under ∗ has generating set S1 = {λ_{l,r,q} : q ∈ F(X), (l,r) ∈ R}, with λ_{l,r,q}=(q^{-1}l^{-1},l,r,q), and defining relations λ_{l,r, v s u} ∗ λ_{s,d,u} = λ_{s,d,u} ∗ λ_{l,r, v d u} for all relation pairs (l,r),(s,d) ∈ R and all u,v ∈ F(X). Here products such as v s u and v d u are computed in F(X), using the paper's convention that words in relation pairs are interpreted in the free group.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 3.4 gives the generators. The displayed relation is exactly the normalized form of the boundary of a Squier 2-cell for two non-overlapping applications of relations (l,r) and (s,d). More generally, a 1-homotopy that inserts or deletes a pair α∘α° of inverse edges does not change the normalized ∗-product: the translated pair becomes ((αd)^{-1}⊲α)∗((αr)^{-1}⊲α°), which is the constant path after expanding the definition of ∗. For a 2-cell with boundary paths α∘β and γ∘δ, normalization gives αλ∗βλ on one side and γλ∗δλ on the other. Writing the general 2-cell in the form used in Definition 3.1 and setting v equal to the combined middle context, these four normalized edges are λ_{l,r, v s u}, λ_{s,d,u}, λ_{s,d,u}, and λ_{l,r, v d u}. Therefore every 2-homotopy imposes one of the displayed relations, and conversely each displayed relation is realized by such a 2-cell. Thus these relations are complete.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = ⟨X : R⟩ be a group presentation, let F=F(X), and let C → F be the free crossed F-module on the function R → F sending (l,r) to l^{-1}r. Then the crossed F-module r : star_1(Sqρ(P)) → F obtained from the reduced Squier complex is isomorphic to C → F. Under this isomorphism, the standard generator (l,r,u) of the free crossed module, corresponding to the relation (l,r) translated by u ∈ F, is sent to λ_{l,r,u}=(u^{-1}l^{-1},l,r,u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define a basis map R → star_1(Sqρ(P)) by (l,r) ↦ λ_{l,r,1}=(l^{-1},l,r,1). Its range under r is l^{-1}r, the same boundary as the basis element in the free crossed module. By the universal property of the free crossed module, there is a crossed-module morphism φ : C → star_1(Sqρ(P)), and equivariance forces φ(l,r,u)=λ_{l,r,u}. This map is bijective on the chosen generating sets. To construct the inverse, send λ_{l,r,u} to (l,r,u). Proposition 3.6 says it suffices to check the defining relations. The relation λ_{l,r, v s u}∗λ_{s,d,u}=λ_{s,d,u}∗λ_{l,r, v d u} maps to (l,r,v s u)(s,d,u)=(s,d,u)(l,r,v d u). In the crossed module C, conjugating (l,r,v s u) by (s,d,u) changes its context by multiplication by ∂(s,d,u)=u^{-1}s^{-1}d u, giving (s,d,u)^{-1}(l,r,v s u)(s,d,u)=(l,r,v d u). Thus the relation holds in C, so the inverse homomorphism is well defined. The two maps are inverse on generators and respect the crossed-module boundary and action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = ⟨X : R⟩ be a presentation of a group G, let θ : F(X) → G be the presentation map, and let N=ker θ. Then there is a short exact sequence of G-modules 0 → π1(Sqρ(P),1) → ⊕_{rel∈R} ZG → N^ab → 0, where the middle term has one free ZG-summand for each defining relation pair in R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The local fundamental group π1(Sqρ(P),1) is the kernel of the range crossed module r : star_1(Sqρ(P)) → F(X). By Theorem 3.7 this crossed module is the free crossed module C(P) → F(X), so the kernel is π(P)=ker ∂ in the notation of Proposition 1.4. Proposition 1.4 supplies the exact sequence 0 → π(P) → C(P)^ab → N^ab → 0 of G-modules. Proposition 1.3 identifies C(P)^ab as the free G-module with basis the relation set R. Substituting π1(Sqρ(P),1) for π(P) gives the stated sequence.

proof_techniques.name: Encoding reductions as a Squier 2-complex
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes applications of presentation relations as directed edges in a complex whose vertices are group elements. An edge (p,l,r,q) means that the relation l=r is applied in the context p _ q, moving from plq to prq. A square 2-cell is attached whenever two relation applications occur in non-overlapping contexts; the square records that the two possible orders of applying them are homotopic.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A matching or cancellation scheme for a word in a free group may be modeled by local cancellation moves in contexts. The quasi-reduced hypothesis excludes the elementary overlap ℓℓ^{-1}ℓ, so a Squier-style complex with cells for independent moves may be especially close to the combinatorics of the matching complex F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Whiskering by left and right contexts
proof_techniques.description: The fundamental groupoid of the reduced Squier complex carries left and right actions by the free group F(X): one multiplies the left or right context of every edge in a path. This turns the groupoid into a regular groupoid and makes it possible to define global products of paths even when their endpoints do not match in the ordinary concatenation sense.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one can try to label subarcs or partial reductions by surrounding word contexts and use whiskering to compare local moves made in different positions. This can reduce dependence on absolute location in the word and isolate the algebraic effect of a cancellation pair.

proof_techniques.name: Commuting independent moves via the equality ∗=⊛
proof_techniques.description: The two products α∗β and α⊛β represent the two possible orders in which one performs path α and path β after inserting the appropriate contexts. The proof of Theorem 3.2 shows first that they agree for single relation applications because of a defining Squier square, then uses induction to extend the equality to arbitrary paths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Crossing resolutions in F_w have two local outcomes and higher cells are built from independent choices. The induction proving that elementary commutations imply arbitrary path commutations may transfer to show that different sequences of resolving or uncrossing operations produce homotopic paths.

proof_techniques.name: Normalizing every path to based elementary generators
proof_techniques.description: Given an edge e, the paper translates it by the inverse of its domain to obtain a based edge eλ in S1, the set of edges starting at the identity. Any path beginning at the identity can then be written as a ∗-product of these based elementary edges. This converts path homotopy questions in the full groupoid into algebraic questions in the star group at the identity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a base object can be chosen for cancellation matchings or reduction diagrams, each local move might be normalized to a standard based move. This could give a presentation of the fundamental group of F_w or of a related reduction complex.

proof_techniques.name: Deriving generators and relations from homotopies
proof_techniques.description: After normalizing paths, the paper analyzes how elementary homotopies affect the resulting products. Insertion or deletion of an inverse edge pair contributes nothing, while passing across a Squier 2-cell gives exactly one Peiffer-type relation among based generators. This yields a complete group presentation for star_1(Sqρ(P)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A route to contractibility of F_w is to extract a combinatorial presentation for its loops from codimension-one/codimension-two local changes and then prove the presentation is trivial. The paper gives a template for doing this systematically.

proof_techniques.name: Crossed-module identification by universal property
proof_techniques.description: The star group of the Squier complex is not merely presented ad hoc; it is identified with the free crossed module associated to the group presentation. The proof defines the map on basis relation edges, obtains a morphism by freeness, and then checks the Squier relations are exactly Peiffer relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matching complex can be related to a rewriting presentation for free reductions, a crossed-module description may turn topological questions about loops into algebraic questions about kernels of boundary maps.

proof_techniques.name: Using relation-module exact sequences as homotopy detectors
proof_techniques.description: The paper identifies π1(Sqρ(P),1) with the kernel in a free crossed module and then embeds it into a free G-module mapping onto the relation module N^ab. This gives an algebraic invariant of the fundamental group of the Squier complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an attempted proof that a complex like F_w is contractible, an analogous exact sequence could either prove simple connectivity by showing the kernel vanishes or reveal obstructions by producing nonzero algebraic classes.

proof_techniques.name: Separating non-overlap and overlap phenomena
proof_techniques.description: The reduced Squier complex in the paper uses only 2-cells for non-overlapping relation applications. In the introduction, the authors recall Pride's augmented Squier complex K+, where extra 2-cells are added to handle overlaps in free-reduction relations xx^{-1}=1=x^{-1}x, so that freely equivalent words have homotopic free-reduction paths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original word is assumed quasi-reduced, precisely excluding subwords ℓℓ^{-1}ℓ where two length-two free cancellations overlap. This suggests that the non-overlap Squier mechanism may suffice locally, while Pride-type overlap cells identify what would need extra handling without the quasi-reduced hypothesis.

other_useful_info: The introduction recalls prior work of Pride, Cremanns-Otto, and Gilbert: for monoid presentations of groups, adding extra 2-cells to the Squier complex to form the Pride complex K+ accounts for possible overlaps in free reductions xx^{-1}=1=x^{-1}x, and in K+ any two edge paths representing the same free equivalence are fixed-endpoint homotopic. Example 3.9 computes the reduced Squier complex for P = ⟨x : xx^{-1}=1⟩, which presents the infinite cyclic group: the vertices are ⟨x⟩, every edge is a loop, and π1(Sqρ(P),1) is a free abelian group of countably infinite rank with generators λ_q indexed by q ∈ Z and action λ_q^x = λ_{q+1}. Corollary 3.10 states that for a finite presentation P of G, π1(Sqρ(P),1) is finitely generated as a G-module if and only if G is of type FP3. Corollary 3.11 reformulates Dyer's L-Cockcroft condition for a subgroup L ≤ G as the vanishing of the induced map π1(Sqρ(P),1) ⊗_L Z → ⊕_{rel∈R} Z(G/L).
why_relevant: Builds Squier-type complexes for group presentations and rewriting systems. This is relevant because the word w is reducible in a free group, and F_w may be interpretable as a space of derivations between reductions.
